items.title: Trump signs executive orders at the White House
items.summary: President Trump signed additional executive orders at the White House on June 22, 2026.
items.details: The White House posted multiple executive orders dated June 22, 2026, among its presidential actions. The signing was recorded in a video published on the White House website.

items.title: GAO reports federal workforce declined in 2025
items.summary: The Government Accountability Office reported the federal workforce fell by more than 11% from December 2024 to January 2026.
items.details: GAO said nearly 378,000 employees separated from 22 major agencies during 2025 while about 127,000 were hired, a net decline of roughly 256,000. Declines ranged from about 1% at Homeland Security to over 45% at the Department of Education.

items.title: Federal Reserve holds interest rate target range
items.summary: The Federal Reserve maintained the federal funds rate target range at 3.5% to 3.75%, effective June 18, 2026.
items.details: The Board of Governors voted unanimously to keep the interest rate on reserve balances at 3.65%. The Federal Open Market Committee directed the New York Fed's trading desk to conduct operations to maintain the target range.

items.title: U.S. and Iran sign preliminary agreement to end war
items.summary: President Trump and Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian signed a memorandum of understanding to halt military operations and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
items.details: The framework, signed June 17, 2026, establishes a 60-day ceasefire window for further negotiations and lifts the U.S. naval blockade. Unresolved issues include Iran's nuclear program, sanctions relief, and frozen assets.

items.title: China sanctions 10 U.S. defense-related companies
items.summary: China announced export restrictions on 10 American military-related firms in response to a U.S. move barring some Chinese tech companies from defense contracts.
items.details: China's Commerce Ministry said Chinese companies would be blocked from exporting dual-use items to the 10 firms, which include drone makers and rare-earth companies. The action followed the Pentagon adding companies including Alibaba and Baidu to its list of firms it links to the Chinese military.

items.title: U.S. intelligence assessment on Israel and Iran deal
items.summary: U.S. intelligence agencies warned the administration that Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu may take steps that could undermine the Iran peace effort, officials said.
items.details: According to current and former U.S. officials cited in the report, the assessment relates to political pressure on Netanyahu over the war in Lebanon. The report was published June 19, 2026.

items.title: Congress passes housing affordability bill
items.summary: Both chambers of Congress passed the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, sending it to the president's desk.
items.details: The Senate passed the bill 85-5 on June 22, and the House cleared it the following day. The package aims to increase housing supply by easing regulations and includes a provision restricting large corporate investors from buying additional single-family homes.

items.title: Five senators voted against the housing bill
items.summary: Five Republican senators voted against the housing legislation, and ten senators did not vote.
items.details: The senators who voted no were Ron Johnson of Wisconsin, Mike Lee of Utah, Rand Paul of Kentucky, Rick Scott of Florida, and Tommy Tuberville of Alabama. Tuberville also voted against an earlier Senate version in March.

items.title: Supreme Court rules in Falun Gong lawsuit against Cisco
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that a group of Falun Gong members cannot proceed with a lawsuit against Cisco Systems.
items.details: The case involved a dozen members of the Falun Gong spiritual movement who said they were imprisoned and tortured in China. The decision was issued June 23, 2026.

items.title: Supreme Court rules in United States v. Hemani
items.summary: The Supreme Court unanimously ruled that prosecuting a marijuana user for owning a gun was inconsistent with the Second Amendment.
items.details: The case stemmed from Ali Hemani, a dual U.S.-Pakistani citizen, whose home search turned up a firearm and marijuana. Justice Neil Gorsuch wrote that the decision is narrow and does not address banning those presently intoxicated from possessing firearms.

items.title: Supreme Court order list adds case and addresses petitions
items.summary: The Supreme Court issued a June order list adding a case for the 2026-27 term and acting on multiple petitions.
items.details: The court added a case concerning the ability to sue federal officials for constitutional violations absent a specific authorizing law. The orders came from the justices' June 18 conference.

items.title: Supreme Court clears Alabama to use congressional map
items.summary: The Supreme Court issued an order allowing Alabama to use a contested congressional map for the 2026 midterms.
items.details: The map, originally drawn in 2023, had previously been barred by a lower court that found it discriminated against Black voters. The order was the first test of the court's earlier Louisiana v. Callais ruling on the Voting Rights Act.

items.title: Republicans hold edge after redistricting battles
items.summary: After a nationwide mid-decade redistricting cycle, an analysis found Republicans could gain about 10 U.S. House seats from new maps.
items.details: Republicans enacted new maps in eight states, while Democrats gained ground mainly in California and Utah. Nearly 145 million people live in states with new congressional districts for the 2026 election.

items.title: New York legislature advances redistricting amendment
items.summary: New York lawmakers passed a constitutional amendment permitting mid-decade redistricting beginning in 2028.
items.details: The amendment, passed June 3, would allow the Legislature greater latitude over the state's Independent Redistricting Commission and remove certain anti-gerrymandering language. It must pass again next year and be approved by voters in 2027 before taking effect.

NH.title: Gov. Ayotte signs 54 bills, vetoes others
NH.summary: New Hampshire Gov. Kelly Ayotte signed 54 bills into law and vetoed several measures during the week of June 19, 2026.
NH.details: Signed bills covered topics including health benefit plan data access and environmental services authorization. Ayotte also vetoed bills including one on the manner of posting town meeting warrants and measures on risk pool operations.

NY.title: State Assembly approves redistricting amendment
NY.summary: The New York State Assembly passed a constitutional amendment to change the state's redistricting process for 2028.
NY.details: The measure requires another legislative vote next session, the governor's sign-off, and voter approval before taking effect. Gov. Kathy Hochul said she supported the legislative effort.

FL.title: Gov. DeSantis signs 25 more bills into law
FL.summary: Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis signed an additional 25 bills, bringing the 2026 total to over 160 laws.
FL.details: The new laws included an appropriation to compensate an injured resident and a measure codifying the University of Florida Diabetes Institute. Several of the laws are set to take effect July 1.

IA.title: Gov. Reynolds acts on final 2026 session bills
IA.summary: Iowa Gov. Kim Reynolds took final action on remaining legislation from the 2026 session, signing bills and issuing line-item vetoes.
IA.details: Signed measures included appropriations bills for transportation, the judicial branch, and economic development. Reynolds line-item vetoed specific provisions from certain appropriations bills.

AL.title: Alabama cleared to use 2023 congressional map
AL.summary: The Supreme Court allowed Alabama to use a congressional map for the 2026 elections that a lower court had found discriminatory.
AL.details: The map, originally drawn in 2023, eliminates a majority-Black district by distributing residents across three districts. The order tests the court's recent ruling raising the bar for Voting Rights Act claims.

CA.title: California's voter-approved congressional map in place for 2026
CA.summary: California's redistricting map, approved by voters as Proposition 50, remains in effect for the 2026 elections after court challenges.
CA.details: The map, championed by Gov. Gavin Newsom, was drawn to counter Texas's Republican redistricting and could yield up to five additional Democratic-leaning seats. The California Republican Party and U.S. Justice Department challenged the map.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable developments from cited sources and does not endorse any party, official, or position.
